modules.title: Introduction of Automata Theory

Conversion from Mealy to Moore and vice versa

modules.title: Types of Finite Automata
topics: Union, intersection, concatenation and closure; 2‑way DFA

modules.title: Grammars

modules.title: Push‑down Automata
topics: Examples of PDA, deterministic and non‑deterministic PDA

modules.title: Turing Machine
topics: Techniques for construction; Universal Turing machine, multitape, multi‑head and multidimensional Turing machines, NP‑complete problems
Decidability and recursively enumerable languages; decidable, undecidable languages, halting problem, Post correspondence problem

experiments: Design a Program for creating machine that accepts three consecutive one.
Design a Program for creating machine that accepts the string always ending with 101.
Design a Program for Mode 3 Machine.
Design a program for accepting decimal number divisible by 2.
Design a program for creating a machine which accepts string having equal no. of 1’s and 0’s.
Design a program for creating a machine which count number of 1’s and 0’s in a given string.
Design a Program to find 2’s complement of a given binary number.
Design a Program which will increment the given binary number by 1.
Design a Program to convert NDFA to DFA.
Design a Program to create PDA machine that accept the well-formed parenthesis.
Design a PDA to accept WCW [R ] where w is any string and W [R] is reverse of that string and C is a Special symbol.
Design a Turing machine that’s accepts the following language a [n] b [n] c [n ] where n>0.
reference_books: Introduction to Automata Theory Language & Computation, Hopcroft & Ullman, Narosa Publication.
Element of the Theory Computation, Lewis &Christors, Pearson.
Theory of Computation, Chandrasekhar & Mishra, PHI.
Theory of Computation, Wood, Harper & Row.
Introduction to Computing Theory, Daniel I-A Cohen, Wiley.

modules.title: DBMS Concepts and Architecture
topics: Introduction, Database approach vs Traditional file accessing approach
ER data model: entities and attributes, entity types
Concept of generalization, aggregation and specialization

Comparison between the three types of models

modules.title: Relational Data Models
topics: Domains, tuples, attributes, relations and characteristics of relations
Keys and key attributes of relation
Relational database schemas and integrity constraints
SQL – DDL and DML, integrity constraints, complex queries, various joins, indexing, triggers, assertions
Relational algebra operations: select, project, join, division, outer union
Tuple‑oriented and domain‑oriented relational calculus and its operations

modules.title: Database Design and Query Optimization
topics: Introduction to normalization, normal forms, functional dependency
Decomposition, dependency preservation and lossless join
Problems with null‑valued and dangling tuples, multivalued dependencies
Query optimization: introduction, steps of optimization, algorithms for select, project and join
Optimization methods – heuristic based and cost‑estimation based

modules.title: Transaction Processing and Concurrency Control
topics: Transaction system, testing of serializability, serializable schedules, conflict & view serializable schedules
Recoverability and recovery from transaction failures, log‑based recovery, checkpoints, deadlock handling
Concurrency control techniques: locking, time‑stamping protocols, validation‑based protocol, multiple granularity
Multiversion concurrency control and recovery with concurrent transactions
Introduction to distributed databases, data mining, data warehousing
Object technology and DBMS, comparative study of OODBMS vs DBMS
Temporal, deductive, multimedia, web & mobile databases

modules.title: Relational DBMS (Oracle/PL‑SQL/MySQL)
topics: Architecture, physical files, memory structures, background processes
Table spaces, segments, extents and blocks; dedicated server and multi‑threaded server
Security, role management, privilege management, profiles, invoker‑defined security model
SQL queries, data extraction from single/multiple tables, equi‑join, non‑equi‑join, self‑join, outer join
Special operators: LIKE, ANY, ALL, EXISTS, IN; hierarchical queries, inline queries, flashback queries
ANSI SQL, anonymous blocks, nested anonymous blocks, branching and looping constructs
Cursor management – nested and parameterized cursors, Oracle exception handling
Stored procedures, IN/OUT/IN OUT parameters, user‑defined functions and their limitations
Triggers, mutating table errors, instead‑of triggers

experiments: Delete duplicate row from the table.
Display the alternate row from table.
Delete alternate row from table.
Update multiple rows using a single update statement.
Find the third highest paid and third lowest paid salary.
Display the 3rd, 4th, 9th rows from table.
Display the ename, which is start with j, k, l or m.
Show all employees who were hired the first half of the month.
Display the three record in the first row and two records in the second row and one record in the third row in a single sql statements.
Write a sql statements for rollback commit and save points.
Write a pl/sql for select, insert, update and delete statements.
Write a pl/sql block to delete a record. If delete operation is successful return 1 else return 0.
Display name, hire date of all employees using cursors.
Display details of first 5 highly paid employees using cursors.
Write a database trigger which fires if you try to insert, update, or delete after 7’o’ clock.
Write a database trigger, which acts just like primary key and does not allow duplicate values.
Create a database trigger, which performs the action of the on delete cascade.
Write a database trigger, which should not delete from emp table if the day is Sunday.

Prepare a small database application (e.g., financial accounting system, railway reservation system, institute timetable management system, student record system, library management system, hospital management system) as a semester project.

experiments: To study basic & user status Unix/Linux commands.
Study & use of commands for performing arithmetic operations with Unix/Linux.
Create a file called wlcc.txt with some lines and display how many lines, words and characters are present in that file.

Append ten more simple lines to the wlcc.txt file created above and split the appended file into 3 parts. Identify the names of the split files, display the contents of each file, and determine the number of lines in the last file.
Given two files each containing names of students, create a program to display only those names that are found in both files.
Create a program to find out the inode number of any desired file.
Study & use of the command for changing file permissions.
Write a pipeline of commands that displays on the monitor as well as saves the information about the number of users using the system at present to a file called usere.ux.
Execute shell commands through the vi editor.
Installation, configuration & customization of Unix/Linux.
Write a shell script that accepts any number of arguments and prints them in reverse order.
Write a shell script to find the smallest of three numbers that are read from the keyboard.
Write a shell script that reports the login of a specified user within one minute after the user logs in; the script automatically terminates if the user does not log in during a specified period.
Installation of SAMBA, Apache, Tomcat.
Implementation of DNS and LDAP services.
Study & installation of firewall & proxy server.
